title: The Macro Minute: Will the Fed sacrifice Main Street to save Wall Street (again)?

Happy Tuesday out there. Team42 at your
skipper here, Darius Dell to present our
macro minute for Tuesday, December 9th,
2025. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note. So, let's hop right in.
Today's key macro question is, will the
Fed sacrifice Main Street to save Wall
Street? Again, the short answer is,
while we are currently unsure of the
answer to this question, one thing is
clear. The Hawks and the Democrats on
the FOMC now have political cover to
push back against calls for doubbish
monetary policy via increased media
scrutiny of the nationwide affordability
crisis. In terms of the analytical
nuance, market attention remains
centered on the Fed's decision and its
outlook for 2026 with the options market
implying a 0.7% birectional swing in US
equities after tomorrow's announcement.
A larger projected move than seen
following recent meetings. While many
investors are focused on the policy rate
guidance, we remain hyperfocused on the
Fed's balance sheet. This week's
gathering marks the first central bank
meeting since the Fed halted
quantitative tightening in response to
indications that systemwide bank
reserves may no longer be ample. Beyond
committing to reinvesting maturities
into treasury bills, the Fed has not yet
clarified its postbalance sheet runoff
plans. The key issue is whether the
financial system retains sufficient
reserves to avoid the recurrent
disruptions that often arise around
quarter end, year end, and increasingly
month end as evidenced by October when
banks reduce repo market engagement to
reinforce cash balances ahead of
regulatory and tax related cutoffs. We
are in line with major Wall Street
institutions and anticipating mounting
year-end money market tightness, which
we've argued may force the Fed to
consider dovish policy actions that
rebuild liquidity buffers in the in the
13 trillion repo market. All told, amid
mounting political pressure to address
the nationwide affordability crisis, the
Trump administration may be inclined to
pursue incremental fiscal stimulus. Even
if they do not, the hawks on the FOMC
may already be less inclined to counter
developing stress in the repo market
with permanent Fed balance sheet
expansion. Temporary balance sheet
expansion is just that, a temporary
solution that does not sufficiently
resolve the issue of not having enough
liquidity sloshing around the financial
sector to sustain the accumulation of
hedge fund leverage and retail investor
margin debt. We reiterate our call that
the 1 to threemonth outlook for asset
markets remains choppy due to the Fed
falling further behind the rate and
liquidity curves amid historic crowded
bullish positioning. Choppy may be an
understatement if the hawks on the FOMC
cause the Fed to push back against
demands for QE in the repo market.
Conversely, if the Fed incrementally
sacrifices Main Street to save Wall
Street by deepening the affordability
crisis with more socialism for the rich,
capitalism for everyone else, monetary
policy, investors should anticipate an
absolute bloodbath for the
administration in the midterm elections,
and the durable headwind that represents
to the 42 macro paradigm C bull market
via a likely dousing of cold water on
the Trump 2.0 deregulation agenda. As
always, members of our global investor
community can trust that our
institutional-grade riskmanagement
overlays, Kiss and Dr. Mo, will help our
portfolios navigate these emergent risks
better than investors attempting to
manage risk without systematic rules or
relying on fundamental research alone.
Transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard
is always wrap up with a question from
our community. This one's titled using
Dr. Mo to invest in any cash app to
invest in a kiss cash allocation. Says,

impressed by the kiss and Dr. Mo
structured datadriven approach and have
invested the family portfolios in Kiss.

in the UK uh and have invested the
current 10% cash allocation in a
sterling pimpco uh quit account which
has low spread costs. I'm just wondering
whether I should use Dr. Mo to invest
these funds in something that offers
some diversification from spy or gold.
One current uh candidate would be
emerging markets. I would revert to KISS
if for example the Bitcoin market vam
supported this whilst the current
Goldilocks environment. So, uh,
excellent question. Uh, so, uh, for
those of you, uh, who may, um, may not
be aware, uh, it's very easy to
customize KISS to your liking. Um, you
know, all you need to do is use the, uh,

either in our publish research or here
in the client dashboard to, uh, you
know, to to figure out exactly what the
amount of exposure you should have to
any of the 70 factors that Dr. Mo
generates uh, proper trade signals for.
If you want u specific guidance on
exactly how to do that, uh what you need
to do as a DIY investor to take these
signals and then use them to uh
diversify your uh KISS portfolio to your
customization and liking. Uh just a
hamburger menu up here at the top right
uh in our uh client dashboard. Scroll
down to the Kiss FAQ section. Uh scroll
down to the sections regarding
customizing KISS. Uh so uh let's see
here. Yeah, right here. What if I'm a
what if I want to implement the KISS
model portfolio but I prefer other
exposures instead of the default SPY
gold or Bitcoin? If I implement a
customized version of KISS, how do I
calculate the exposures to my preferred
uh ETFs? So obviously we have the
answers to those questions here uh to

which is riskmanaged 60 3010 stocks go

investment objectives for their risk
tolerance uh etc. Uh so uh hopefully you
guys found that helpful. We'll wrap it

macro minute for Tuesday December 9th

will catch you back here tomorrow.
Cheers.
